coredata.dc:title: Robust polymer electrolytes with fast ion-transport nanochannels constructed by carbon dots for long lifespan Li metal batteries

coredata.dc:description: As an anode material in energy storage systems, Li metal has exceptional merits, including high specific capacity, low density, and low redox potential. However, challenges such as lithium dendrites and unstable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) hinder the practical application of lithium metal batteries (LMBs). Solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs), notably PVDF-HFP, are expected to overcome the above problems because of their mechanical merits and safe features, but their limited ion transportation behaviors result in insufficient ionic conductivity at room temperature. Herein, we invent an approach to prepare high performance electrolytes composed by porous PVDF-HFP and functionalized carbon dots (CDs). Such porous PVDF-HFP polymer membranes are created by a subtle phase inversion process, possessing a sponge-like structure, vertical lithium-ion transport channels and CDs decorated surfaces, thus able to uptake the liquid electrolyte (LE) to form gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs). The CDs fillers are produced in large scale with adjustable surface states, which can enhance Li salts disassociation to release free Li ions. As a result, the optimal conductivity of the as-prepared GPEs at room temperature is up to 8.7 mS cm−1 and the lithium transference number reaches 0.64. Our GPEs endow Li symmetric batteries with very stable SEI and long cycling life over 2200 h, and help realize high capacities and retention rates of the LMBs using LiFePO4(LFP) or LiCoO2(LCO) cathodes.

(a) The schematic phase inversion preparation for the porous GPE membranes. SEM images of (b) the cast PVDF-HFP, (c) the phase inversion derived PVDF-HFP and (d) PVDF-HFP/CDs films. (e) The electrolyte uptake ratios and the porosities of different membranes. (f) The conductivities of different membranes at room temperature. (g) The conductivities of PVDF-HFP/U-CDs films with different contents of U-CDs. (h) The schematic comparison between the GPEs derived from three polymers: the cast PVDF-HFP (solid polymer), the phase inversion derived PVDF-HFP (stacked polymer dots), and the PVDF-HFP/CDs (polymer-CDs columns).

(a) Cycling performance and (b) rate performance of Li||PVDF-HFP||LFP, Li||PVDF-HFP/N-CD||LFP and Li||PVDF-HFP/U-CDs||LFP cells, respectively. (c) Charging-discharging curves at different rates of Li||PVDF-HFP/U-CD||LFP cell, respectively. (d) Electrochemical windows of different GPEs. (e) Cycling performance of Li||PVDF-HFP||LCO, Li||PVDF-HFP/N-CD||LCO and Li||PVDF-HFP/U-CDs||LCO cells at 1C, respectively.

head.ce:title: Robust polymer electrolytes with fast ion-transport nanochannels constructed by carbon dots for long lifespan Li metal batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Molecular Catalysis and Innovative Materials, Fudan University, Shanghai 200438, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Molecular Catalysis and Innovative Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Molecular Catalysis and Innovative Materials, Fudan University, Shanghai 200438, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an anode material in energy storage systems, Li metal has exceptional merits, including high specific capacity, low density, and low redox potential. However, challenges such as lithium dendrites and unstable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) hinder the practical application of lithium metal batteries (LMBs). Solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs), notably PVDF-HFP, are expected to overcome the above problems because of their mechanical merits and safe features, but their limited ion transportation behaviors result in insufficient ionic conductivity at room temperature. Herein, we invent an approach to prepare high performance electrolytes composed by porous PVDF-HFP and functionalized carbon dots (CDs). Such porous PVDF-HFP polymer membranes are created by a subtle phase inversion process, possessing a sponge-like structure, vertical lithium-ion transport channels and CDs decorated surfaces, thus able to uptake the liquid electrolyte (LE) to form gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs). The CDs fillers are produced in large scale with adjustable surface states, which can enhance Li salts disassociation to release free Li ions. As a result, the optimal conductivity of the as-prepared GPEs at room temperature is up to 8.7 mS cm−1 and the lithium transference number reaches 0.64. Our GPEs endow Li symmetric batteries with very stable SEI and long cycling life over 2200 h, and help realize high capacities and retention rates of the LMBs using LiFePO4(LFP) or LiCoO2(LCO) cathodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Li metal is regarded as the “Holy Grail” anode material [1] for its outstanding properties, including its high specific capacity (3860mAh g−1) and low redox potential (−3.04 V vs SHE). And thus, LMBs are the potential next-generation energy storage systems with high energy densities. However, the commercial LEs for Li-ion batteries are volatile and flammable, which are not suitable for LMBs because of safety issues, Li dendrite growth and long-term interface stability [2,3]. Recently, various solid-state electrolytes and quasi-solid electrolytes are developed to solve the above problems, among which some inorganic electrolytes possess high ionic conductivities but poor interface impedances, especially their unmanageable brittleness [4,5]. In contrast, polymer electrolytes turn out to be a practical choice due to their high workability, great interfacial adaptivity and less concern on safety issues [6,7]. Unfortunately, the most widely investigated polymers, including polyethylene (PEO) [8], polyacrylonitrile (PAN) [9], and poly (propylene carbonate) (PPC) [10], are restricted by their insufficient room temperature ionic conductivities (<10−5 S cm−1) and low cation transference numbers (0.2 ∼ 0.4) [11]. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and its copolymer with hexafluopropylene (PVDF-HFP) outperform these counterparts with the ability of boosting the dissociation of Li salts and avoiding uneven lithium flux. These advantages arise from their highly fluorinated polymer structure and high dielectric constant of about 11.7 [12–14]. In fact, the highly crystallized structure of PVDF and PVDF-HFP analogs have low donor numbers and thus lack of ion hooking sites, implying that the ion conducting behavior greatly depends on the residual solvents in the electrolytes. Therefore, constructing the highly porous structure and introducing the stable liquid phases are essential to endow the PVDF based GPEs with sufficient conductivities [15–19].

Various fillers have been introduced into PVDF based polymer electrolytes to enhance their conductivities and cation transference numbers [20–22], including inorganic inert fillers such as SiO2 [23,24], Al2O3 [25,26], CuO [27], and Mg(ClO4)2 [28], inorganic active fillers such as Li7La3Zr2O12 [29], Li6.4La3Zr2Ta0.6O12 [30–32], and Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 [33], and some functional fillers like GO [34,35] and MOF [36]. This strategy is limited by the uneven dispersity of the fillers and the expensiveness of some ceramic fillers [37]. Besides, the composition and morphology of functional fillers is vital for the lithium transference performance and the mechanical strength of GPEs [38]. Thus, ideal fillers should combine with high dispersity in common solvents, good compatibility with the polymer matrix and appropriate interaction with the lithium ions. CDs are nanoparticles with graphitic cores and highly functionalized surfaces [39,40], which have been used as fillers for polymer electrolytes to accelerate lithium salt dissociation without creating tortuous ion transport pathways [41–44]. Therefore, CDs with properly designed chemical structure are beneficial to boost the electrochemical performance of the energy storage systems [45,46].

Herein, we prepare a new type of PVDF-HFP based GPEs with high conductivities and Li transference numbers through introducing CDs to build up fast lithium pathways after a special membrane producing process. The present CDs are fabricated from cheap raw materials via simple routes, which show highly functionalized structure and strong interactions with lithium salts. Highly porous PVDF-HFP membranes are produced through a mild phase inversion process with simultaneous CDs incorporation. The as-prepared PVDF-HFP/CDs membranes can adsorb sufficient LE to exhibit an improved ion conductivity (8.7 mS cm−1) at room temperature. The optimized PVDF-HFP/CDs membrane possesses a high Li transference number (0.64) and help form a rich LiF composed SEI at the electrode surface. As a result, such a composite electrolyte exhibits an ultralong cycle life over 2200 h when assembled in a Li symmetric battery working at a current density of 0.25 mA cm−2 and an areal capacity of 0.25mAh cm−2. The corresponding Li||LFP battery shows an initial capacity of 125.8mAh g−1 and a retention rate of 89.7 % after 300 cycles. The GPEs can also couple with the high voltage cathode LCO to exhibit a capacity of 130.0mAh g−1 and a retention rate of 95.07 % after 100 cycles.

3.0 g of urea and 3.0 g of citric acid were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water in a 150 mL beaker. After a microwave treatment for 5 min, 80 mL of water was added to dissolve the generated CDs. The final products were collected after a filtration and a freeze-dry treatment subsequently. For the sake of long-term storage, the CDs were dissolved in N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) with a concentration of 6 wt%. The as-prepared CDs were marked as U-CDs.

For comparation, another kind of CDs were synthesized on the basis of the literature [47]. 6.0 g of citric acid, 1.62 g of ethylenediamine and 5 mL of DMF were put in a PTFE-lined autoclave for 5 h heating at 160 ℃. After the solvothermal reaction, the product was precipitated by ethanol for centrifugation, followed by repeated washing with ethanol and centrifugation. Finally, the sample was dried at 80 ℃, and the obtained powder was labelled as N-CDs.

0.3 g of PVDF powder, 0.1 g of CDs/DMF solution and 4 mL of N-Methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) were mixed to form a homogeneous solution. After pouring the solution into a specific PTFE container, a film formed spontaneously at the bottom. Several hours later, the generated film was taken out and immersed in the deionized water to eliminate residual NMP solvent through a solvent exchange progress. Then the membrane was dried at room temperature under air blowing, and then cut into round pieces with a diameter of 16 mm. These round membranes were transferred into a glove box, where the GPEs were obtained through immersing the membrane in a commercial MJS-LB303 electrolyte for 5 sec before measurements. The MJS-LB303 electrolyte is composed of 1 M of LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) (the solvent volume ratio is 1:1:1).

A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS GeminiSEM 300) were employed to investigate the nanostructured materials. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D2 Endeavor) with Cu-Ka radiation (λ = 0.1541 nm, 40 kV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of powder samples and electrolytes were collected on Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS10 spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were measured on a Horiba Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) data was collected on a thermal analysis instrument (Hitachi STA200) with a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1 from 30 ℃ to 800 ℃ in N2.

The electrolyte resistance was acquired at an electrochemical workstation (CHI660 E), where the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy testing of symmetric SS||GPE||SS cells was conducted with the frequency ranging from 1000 kHz to 0.1 Hz. The electrochemical window was measured by a linear sweep voltage (LSV) measurement on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660 E). LSV testing was performed in a voltage window of 2.5 ∼ 6.5 V with a scan rate of 0.1 mV/s. For the full cell measurements, LFP and LCO cathodes were prepared by mixing the active material (80 wt%) with super-P conductor (10 wt%) and polyvinylidene fluoride binder (10 wt%) in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone, scraping onto an aluminum plate, and then drying at 60 ℃. The mass loading of the active materials was about 1 ∼ 2 mg cm−2. 2032 coin-shaped Li||Cu half cells, Li||Li symmetric cells, and Li||cathode full cells were assembled respectively in an argon-filled glove box for the subsequent electrochemical tests. The discharge/charge cycles of all electrode materials were measured by a multichannel cell test system (Land ct2001a) under room temperature. The batteries using LFP cathodes were tested between 2.5 ∼ 3.8 V, while those using LCO cathodes were measured between 2.5 ∼ 4.2 V.

In order to study the relationship between the composition of CDs fillers and the electrochemical performance of the resulting GPEs, two kinds of popular CDs are synthesized and added in the polymer electrolytes, respectively. One is U-CDs made from citric acid and urea by a microwave synthesis, that is rapid and massive without safety concerns on high pressure (Fig. 1
a and Fig. S1). The other is N-CDs synthesized through the classic hydrothermal reaction between citric acid and ethylenediamine. XRD diffraction patterns display a broad peak around 18° revealing the highly amorphous carbon phases of U-CDs (Fig. 1c) and N-CDs (Fig. S2). More evidentially, the TEM images clearly reveal the core–shell structures for both samples in Fig. 1b (U-CDs) and Fig. S3 (N-CDs). The cores have a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the d spacing of the graphene (100) planes [47]. Besides, the TEM results illustrate the diameter distribution of these nanoparticles (Fig. S4), in which U-CDs are smaller (about 2.4 nm) than N-CDs (about 2.9 nm) and U-CDs have the narrower diameter distribution. Therefore, U-CDs can be dispersed more homogeneously in the polymer matrix. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 1d prove the rich functional groups on CDs, which may interact with lithium salts. The board peak at 3431 and 3207 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH, respectively. The absorption bands located at 1713, 1615, and 1189 cm−1 are aligned to the stretching vibration of −C = O, −C = N, −C-N functional groups, respectively. The existence of –CH bending vibration band is shown by the peak at 1362 cm−1 [48]. The FTIR spectra of N-CDs in Fig. S5 exhibit similar absorption peaks, indicating that both kinds of CDs possess the similar surface groups.

To compare the surface states of U-CDs and N-CDs in depth, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out. Three peaks at 285, 400, 532 eV in the full spectra (Fig. 1e) represent the signals of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s electron, respectively. The ratio of the C, N, O elements in U-CDs is figured out to be 56.99:18.17:24.85, while that in N-CDs is 64.60:13.66:21.74. In general, a high amount of heteroatoms in the CDs results in a low electronic conductive nature [49], and both N and O species are the interaction sites with Li ions. The high resolution C 1s spectra of U-CDs (Fig. 1f) can be divided into four parts, indicating the presence of C = C/C-C (284.80 eV), C-N/C-O (286.78 eV), C = O (288.12 eV), COOH (290.2 eV) structures, respectively[50]. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 1g) show the existence of pyridinic N (399.27 eV), pyrrolic N (399.98 eV), and graphitic N (401.32 eV), respectively [51,52]. The deconvoluted XPS results of and N-CDs are shown in Fig. S6, and the detailed ratios of both CDs are compared in Tables S1 and S2. It should be mentioned that the total content of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N in U-CDs (22.14 %) is higher than that in N-CDs (15.28 %). According to previous calculation results [53,54], such N species are beneficial for Li uniform distribution on anode surface through strong interactions with Li+ ions. The structure differences between U-CDs and N-CDs are also reflected by their photoluminescent (PL) spectra in Fig. S7. Although both of them exhibit the excitation-dependent fluorescence features [55], U-CDs and N-CDs show their PL maximum intensity at 538 nm (excited at 420 nm) and 468 nm (excited at 390 nm), respectively. Since the longer emission wavelength corresponds to the larger conjugated structure, the PL results suggest that the U-CDs have more conjugated heteroatoms and rings than N-CDs.

PVDF-HFP based membranes are commonly produced through solution-casting or electrospinning method [56,57], but these methods always cause bad interfacial contact or poor affinity to electrolytes. Since phase inversion methods are beneficial to create pores in the membranes [58], we prepare the polymer-CDs membranes through a mild spontaneous phase inversion process with four steps (Fig. 2
a). To compare with the CD-free PVDF-HFP, the CDs filled polymers after phase inversion are named as PVDF-HFP/U-CDs and PVDF-HFP/N-CDs respectively. As control, the casting method derived polymer is named as the cast PVDF-HFP. The traditional phase inversion behavior often occur fiercely after addition of a non-solvent, in which polymers transfer quickly from a solvent into a solid state with a compact and closed surface [59,60]. In our present study, the humidity in the atmosphere act as the non-solvent. And thus, the phase inversion process takes place in a very mild way, ending up in the form of precipitate as illustrated in step 2 of Fig. 2a. After the membranes generating procedure, the solvent exchange is conducted between water and NMP (step 3), which helps form vertical channels in the polymer matrix [61]. Besides, the polar H2O molecules are beneficial to create polarized channel surfaces [62]. Although both CDs are water soluble, most of them stay stably in the polymer matrix based on their strong affinity to PVDF-HFP. After the solvent exchange, the polymer films are photoluminescent, while the NMP aqueous solution has almost no fluorescence. Subsequently, the films are dried thoroughly and cut into round pieces. In Fig. S8, these polymer membranes are white before soaking liquid electrolytes. After immersion in the electrolytes for 5 sec (step 4), all of them turn into transparent GPEs.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images exhibit the different surface states of these samples. The cast PVDF-HFP film (Fig. 2b) is compact and flat, while the phase inversion derived PVDF-HFP film (Fig. 2c) and PVDF-HFP/CDs film (Fig. 2d) are porous. Without CDs fillers, the PVDF-HFP sub-globose particles have a random distribution of crystal sizes. In contrast, the surface of the polymer-CDs membranes displays a sponge-like porous interface with multiple vertical channels. From the side view, the morphology differences between these samples are dramatic (Fig. S9). The PVDF-HFP film made from solution casting is totally compact, while its counterpart made from phase inversion is accumulated by tiny PVDF-HFP spheres. In a sharp contrast, the PVDF-HFP/N-CDs film looks like cauliflowers, while the PVDF-HFP/U-CDs film appears to be regularly arranged needle mushrooms. Therefore, our slow phase inversion method provides a unique environment for polymer recrystallization, where CDs play important roles in constructing channels in the polymer films. In addition, the EDS mapping of the N element in PVDF-HFP/U-CDs (Fig. S10) illustrates that N-containing CDs fillers are dispersed homogeneously in the polymer film. Thermal stability is vital for the practical use of gel polymer electrolytes, and the thermogravimetric (TG) analyses of the membranes are conducted in Fig. S11. All the membranes do not exhibit noticeable weight loss before 450 ℃, depicting that the residential solvent and moisture are previously eliminated through the fabricating procedure. The thermostability under the restricted working conditions are affirmed in the meantime. In the relative DSC data (Fig. S12), the first heat absorbing peaks locate at 140.1, 134.2, 142.6 ℃ for PVDF-HFP, PVDF-HFP/N-CDs and PVDF-HFP/U-CDs respectively, which are comparable to the commercial PE or PP separators.

Fig. 2e–f compare the above four samples from three aspects: porosities, uptake amounts of the electrolyte, and conductivities of the resulting GPEs. The porosity test was carried out by immersing the sample in 1-butanol for 2 h, followed by calculation according to the weights of the dry membrane (m0) and the wet membrane (m1).[63] Obviously, the phase inversion produced samples have much larger porosities (75 ∼ 80 %) than the cast one (46 %), which suggests that liquid electrolyte can be saturated in the porosity.[64] As a result, the uptake amounts of the electrolyte by the three samples (210 ∼ 250 %) are more than 10 times over that by the cast sample. As for the ionic conductivities measured at room temperature, the data of the above four samples are 2.38, 5.80, 6.58 and 8.70 mS cm−1, respectively. It is clear that not only the porous structure but also the CDs filler determine the conductivity of the sample. To study the influence of the CDs filler, PVDF-HFP with different contents of U-CDs are prepared and measured. Their conductivities in Fig. 2g show that the content of 5 wt% is optimal. Excessive amount of CDs fillers may lessen the ability of lithium movement. Hence, the amount of CDs addition is fixed at 5 wt% of the whole membrane for the subsequent electrochemical experiments. Fig. 2h compares the above three kinds of GPEs schematically. The cast PVDF-HFP is a solid polymer film, which have very low porosity and can only adsorb little LE to form GPE. And thus, its conductivity is the lowest. The phase inversion derived PVDF-HFP is actually stacked polymer dots with disordered and sinuous pores. Although its LE adsorption amount is larger, the transport of lithium ions in it remains slow and difficult. In contrast, the PVDF-HFP/CDs sample is composed of columnar polymer arrays which are vertical to the lithium plate and decorated with CDs. After adsorption a large amount of LE, the obtained GPE has vertical channels for Li ions transport, and the CDs can help disassociation of lithium salt to increase Li ion transference number.

The well-designed CDs fillers are able to optimize Li-ion solvating environment [65], in which more free Li ions are released through the interactions between CDs and the lithium salt. In general, the FTIR peak of the P-F bonds absorption in the range of 810 ∼ 890 cm−1 can be analyzed to study the subtle interactions between LiPF6 and the fillers. This FTIR band is fitted into three parts: the uncoordinated anion at about 844 cm−1, and the ion pairs at about 834 cm−1 and 877 cm−1 because of the disrupted symmetry [66]. Four samples are compared in this way (Fig. 3 a–d), including the liquid electrolyte (LE), the LE immersed PVDF-HFP, PVDF-HFP/N-CDs and PVDF-HFP/U-CDs, respectively. After deconvolution of the FTIR band, the percentage of free ions of the above samples are 57.4 % (LE), 59.5 % (PVDF-HFP), 74.6 % (PVDF-HFP/N-CDs) and 84.5 % (PVDF-HFP/U-CDs), respectively. This result confirms that CDs fillers have strong ability to disassociate lithium salts, which benefit ion transference in GPEs. Li ion transference number (tLi+) is the key parameter to evaluate the transference ability and rate capability of lithium batteries, which is carried out by measuring the impedance and current changes under a small polarized potential (typically 10 mV). Fig. 3e–h list the tLi+ measurements of the four samples, and the tLi+ results are 0.27 (LE), 0.34 (PVDF-HFP), 0.61 (PVDF-HFP/N-CDs) and 0.64 (PVDF-HFP/U-CDs), respectively. Obviously, the CDs fillers are able to modulate the chemical environment for the lithium salt in the electrolyte, thus increasing the tLi+ significantly. Such an ability is based on the interactions between the lithium salt and the CDs surface sites including –COOH, pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N, etc.

High tLi+ ensures Li-ions dominate charge carrying behavior instead of a dual ion process [67], which will facilitate the long cycle performance of the Li batteries. To verify this point, Li symmetric batteries are assembled with different electrolytes and tested under 0.5 mA cm−2 and 0.5mAh cm−2 for 100 h, respectively. Their impendences are measured after every 10 cycles to evaluate the situation of the anode-electrolyte interfaces. As shown in Fig. S13, the battery using LE shows unstable impendence changes, indicating the development of the Li-LE interfaces is out of control. On the contrary, the other three batteries using GPEs display the same trend of the impendence changes which decrease sharply in the first 20 cycles and then change slowly in the following 30 cycles, indicating both the SEI formation process and the reversible stripping/planting process are stable. In Fig. 4 , the SEM images of the Li metal anode after 100 h Li stripping/planting demonstrate that the LE-contacted Li anode surface is loose and uneven, while the GPEs-contacted Li plates are dense and flat, especially the PVDF-HFP/U-CDs contacted one. Obviously, PVDF-HFP based GPEs are able to control Li deposition homogeneously and suppressing the dendrite growth, especially when CDs fillers are incorporated. These Li anodes are further investigated by XPS measurements. The high resolution XPS spectra of F 1s show that the composition ratios of CF3 (687.1 eV) and LiF (684.9 eV) change gradually [68,69]. In comparison with the LE-related sample, the CDs incorporation increases the LiF content in the anode surface greatly. LiF is widely regarded as an excellent Li dendrite inhibitor due to its fast Li diffusion nature and high Young’s Modulus, so it is an ideal composition for a dense and robust solid electrolyte interface (SEI) [70,71]. In the high resolution XPS spectra of Li 1s, the LiF component can be analyzed by the fitted peak at 56.2 eV [72], which also prove that CDs incorporation results high contents of LiF in the SEI. In Fig. S14, the deconvoluted C1s spectra show the complex composition of SEI, including −COOLi, −CF3, −C = O, −C-O, −C-N, etc. These organic components as well as those inorganic LiF and Li2O are derived from the decomposition of the carbonate electrolyte and the Li salt [73]. By comparing the above XPS data, it is also uncovered that the splitting of the carbonate species can be prevented by the CDs fillers, which will significantly enhance the stability of Li batteries.

Li symmetric batteries assembled with PVDF-HFP/U-CDs GPEs exhibit long-term stability owing to the controlled lithium flux and the chemically stable SEI. As shown in Fig. 5
a, a typical battery works at 0.25 mA cm−2 and 0.25 mAh cm−2 for 2200 h without obvious overpotential increase. However, the batteries employing PVDF-HFP and PVDF-HFP/N-CDs GPEs display sharp potential rises after 1300 and 1800 h, respectively. In Fig. 5b, the Li||PVDF-HFP/U-CDs||Li symmetric battery shows 750 h steady Li stripping/plating performance at 0.5 mA cm−2 and 0.5mAh cm−2, which is outstanding among the reported long-term stability of Li batteries (Table S3). In Fig. S15, four Nyquist plots Li symmetric batteries, representing the Celgard commercial membranes, PVDF-HFP, PVDF-HFP/N-CDs and PVDF-HFP/U-CDs films, show the initial impendence of 681, 293, 372, and 488 Ω, respectively. Since the CDs in the polymer matrix create hierarchical structures, the initial impendence and overpotential reveal some subtle rise. However, as shown in Fig. 5c, after 1000 h symmetric battery test, the overpotentials of the above four samples are 63.55, 60.45, 46.81 and 32.86 mV, which prove that CDs help create more stable electrolyte–electrode interfaces for Li ion transmission. The low overpotential of PVDF-HFP/U-CDs based batteries stay steady throughout the symmetric battery tests, while the other groups cannot keep working normally after 1700 h and 2000 h, respectively (Fig. 5 a ∼ c). In addition, Li||Cu semi-batteries are also assembled to evaluate the cycling performance of these electrolytes, respectively. In Fig. S16, the PVDF-HFP/U-CDs GPEs assembled cell outperformed the other groups with a Coulombic efficiency of 92.78 % after 150 cycles.

To test the cycling performance of the Li metal batteries at room temperature, Li||GPE||LFP(LiFePO4) full cells are assembled and tested at 1C (1C = 178mAh g−1). Li||PVDF-HFP/U-CDs||LFP cells exhibit the highest initial capacity of 125.8mAh g−1 and a retention rate of 89.7 % after 300 cycles (Fig. 6
a). Fig. 6b and c display the better rate performance of Li||PVDF-HFP/U-CDs||LFP cells at 158.2, 151.3, 141.3, 128.5, 108.7mAh g−1 under 0.1C,0.2C, 0.5C, 1C and 2C, respectively. Under the above test conditions the IR drops are 46, 64, 126, 265 and 278 mV, respectively. In contrast, severe capacity decays occur on the samples using PVDF-HFP and PVDF-HFP/N-CDs GPEs. Moreover, the comparison with other reported polymer electrolytes in Li metal cells are listed in supporting information Table S4 in which our polymer electrolytes were comparable with several reported articles.

According to the linear sweep voltammetry tests in Fig. 6d, PVDF-HFP/U-CDs and PVDF-HFP/N-CDs electrolytes exhibit the higher electrochemical windows at 4.96 and 4.45 V respectively, which enable them to work with higher voltage cathode materials to pursue the higher energy density.[74] Thus, galvanostatic charge–discharge tests are carried out for the Li||GPE||LCO(LiCoO2) full cells with the operating voltage of 2.5 ∼ 4.2 V. Besides, in Figs. S17 and S18, the Nyquist plots of different Li||GPE||LFP and Li||GPE||LCO full cells confirm that the presence of CDs in Li metal batteries makes no significant difference in the resistance of the whole batteries.

In Fig. 6e, the battery with PVDF-HFP GPE shows a rapid capacity decay after 10 cycles of activation, while the Li||PVDF-HFP/N-CDs||LCO possesses a capacity of 130.0mAh g−1 and retention rate of 95.07 % after 100 cycles under 1C (1C = 140mAh g−1). Such improved cycling performance indicates that the CDs fillers make a remarkable contribution to meet the practical demands for high energy density electrochemical devices.

Two kinds of PVDF-HFP/CDs GPEs are prepared through a new phase inversion route for LMBs, including Li||Li symmetric cells, Li||Cu semi cells, Li||LFP and Li||LCO full cells. These batteries work well at room temperature and some of them possess long life and high energy densities. CDs fillers make a key contribution to the above achievements, because they induce the formation of vertical ion-transport channels in the polymer matrix and their functional surface groups are benefit for Li ion solvation structures, resulting in good ionic conductivities of the GPEs, high tLi+ values, robust LiF-rich SEI and remarkable stability. These electrochemical performances are much better than those of the cast PVDF-HFP, the CDs free PVDF-HFP, and the commercial LE. And the PVDF-HFP/U-CDs perform even better than PVDF-HFP/N-CDs, because the former has more active sites to interact with Li salts so as to release more free Li ions. Our present work confirms the important roles of CDs fillers in constructing robust GPEs for lithium batteries with excellent performance, and provides instructions for design multifunctional CDs for this aim.
